findings.category: A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
findings.title: D18 redaction regex does not match non-prefixed private keys or 65-byte signatures
findings.description:
D13/D18 redactor is specified as matching `0x[0-9a-fA-F]{64}` (32-byte hex with explicit `0x` prefix). This pattern has documented gaps that defeat the stated goal of preventing accidental relayer-key leaks in logs and error stacks:

1. Private keys serialized WITHOUT `0x` prefix (e.g., `Buffer.from(key).toString('hex')`, `key.replace(/^0x/, '')`, or any custom logger transform) will pass through unredacted. This is the common shape for env-var values (`PAYWALL_RELAYER_KEY=abc123...` without 0x), keystore exports, and many `viem`/`ethers` error-internal stringifications.
2. Full ECDSA signatures from `signTypedData` are 65 bytes (130 hex chars) — `0x` followed by 130 hex chars does NOT match `{64}` anchored. So full signatures pass through unredacted; while signatures themselves aren't secret, they enable replay correlation if logs are exfiltrated.
3. The regex is anchored on length 64 exactly; any whitespace/decoration around the key (e.g., quoted JSON like `"0xABCD..."`) still matches, but raw concatenation in error messages (`Error: invalid key 0xABCD...DEF more text`) only matches if the next char after 64 hex is non-hex — works for hex but a 128-char hex string would match the FIRST 64 chars only and leave the trailing 64 visible.

Given D13 explicitly lists `util.inspect`, `pino`, `winston`, `structuredClone`, AND error-stack capture as redaction targets, and D18 routes ten distinct security events through the same redactor, a missed key in any one of these paths is a credential disclosure. This is the only mechanism preventing relayer-key leakage at scale.
findings.impact: Relayer private key written to logs in any of these forms is exfiltrated to whoever reads the logs (Datadog ingestion, log aggregator, support-ticket attachments, error-monitoring like Sentry). Relayer EOA holds USDC for gas — full balance drained on key compromise. Also: a future maintainer who introduces a new logging path (e.g., raw error.cause stringification) silently breaks the redaction guarantee with no test signal.
findings.recommendation:
Tighten the redactor spec:
1. Pattern set, not single regex. Redact ANY of: `(?:0x)?[0-9a-fA-F]{64}` (case-insensitive, optional 0x), `(?:0x)?[0-9a-fA-F]{128}` (signature without recovery), `(?:0x)?[0-9a-fA-F]{130}` (full signature with v).
2. Require word-boundary anchors so 128-char hex doesn't match only the first 64.
3. Add explicit unit tests (already partially in Task 6 — extend): no-`0x` prefix, mid-string, full signature, base64-encoded buffer of the key, error.cause chain.
4. Encode the OpaqueRelayerKey as an opaque symbol-keyed value such that `String(key)`, `key.toString()`, `JSON.stringify(key)`, `util.inspect(key)`, AND `Buffer.from(key as any).toString('hex')` all return `'[REDACTED]'`. Make the underlying-bytes accessor a non-enumerable Symbol that throws on any access path other than the canonical one in `settle.ts`.
5. Add a `SecurityLogger` redaction-conformance test: feed each of the 10 event payloads through a real `pino`/`winston` instance and grep the serialized output for any 64+ hex sequence — fail if found.
findings.cwe: CWE-532 (Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File), CWE-200 (Information Exposure)

findings.title: Rogue clone Risks entry states the defense incorrectly
findings.description:
The Risks-table entry for `Rogue clone deployment` claims: 'A vault that wasn't deployed by the canonical factory will have `factory != NETWORKS[id].factoryAddress` and middleware skips it.' This implies middleware reads `vault.factory()` at runtime and rejects mismatches. It does not. Per Solution step 3 and step 7d, middleware uses `factory.computeVaultAddress(developerEoa)` against the canonical factory baked into `NETWORKS[id].factoryAddress` — it never reads `vault.factory()`.

The actual defense is structural: the canonical factory is the ONLY contract that can deploy a clone at the address `Clones.predictDeterministicAddress(impl, bytes20(dev), canonicalFactory)`. CREATE2 address depends on the deployer; a rogue contract deploying a clone with the same salt+impl produces a DIFFERENT address that the middleware never asks about. So the actual security claim is: 'rogue clones exist at addresses the middleware never references; the middleware-computed vault is provably canonical.'

The spec's stated reasoning is misleading and could mislead a future implementer into removing the `computeVaultAddress` step (thinking the runtime back-pointer check is the real defense) — at which point the vault address comes from somewhere user-controlled, and the rogue-clone vector reopens.
findings.impact: If a future maintainer reads the Risks entry, believes the `factory` back-pointer is the defense, and removes the canonical-factory derivation in middleware (e.g., 'optimization': accept a `payTo` from config directly to skip the CREATE2 compute) — the entire per-developer-vault security property collapses. An attacker deploys a rogue clone at a chosen address, convinces a developer to set it as their vault, and intercepts payments.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite the Risks entry to state the actual defense: 'CREATE2 address depends on (impl, salt, deployer). The canonical factory address is baked into `NETWORKS[id].factoryAddress` at package build time. Middleware ALWAYS derives `payTo` via `Clones.predictDeterministicAddress(impl, salt=bytes20(dev), deployer=canonicalFactory)` — the address it returns is provably a clone deployed by the canonical factory (or empty / not-yet-deployed, caught by the `vaults[dev] != 0` check). Rogue clones with the same salt+impl deployed by a non-canonical factory exist at different CREATE2 addresses and are unreachable from the payment flow.' Optionally add a vault-side `factory == address` sanity check as defense-in-depth, but state explicitly that the address-derivation is the primary defense.
findings.cwe: CWE-1059 (Insufficient Technical Documentation), CWE-345 (Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity)

findings.title: D17 lacks compile-time immutability enforcement for `developer` and `factory`
findings.description:
D17 acknowledges that `immutable` cannot be used on values set in `initialize()`. The mitigation is: 'no setter exists' + NatSpec documentation + an ABI test that asserts no `setDeveloper`/`setFactory` selectors exist (Task 5). This is purely social/test-time enforcement.

Problems with this stance:
1. A future maintainer can add `function setDeveloper(address)` and the ABI test catches it ONLY if the maintainer hasn't also updated the ABI test (which they're likely to do as part of 'adding a feature').
2. The ABI test is necessary but not sufficient — a maintainer could add a function with a different name (`migrateDeveloper`, `rotateOwner`) that mutates the same storage slot. The ABI test catches only the two specific selectors named.
3. Storage layout: `developer` and `factory` occupy two slots that are easy to overwrite via a future `delegatecall` to a misbehaving extension (vault doesn't have delegatecall today, but D15/D16/D17 add 'social' guarantees that the *contract* won't grow — the spec doesn't lock the structural shape).

The alternative the spec dismisses — 'logical immutables via `private` storage + view getters' — is in fact NOT equivalent. The cleanest enforcement: introduce a single `bytes32 private constant _IMMUTABLE_GUARD = keccak256('immutable')` slot pattern, OR follow OpenZeppelin's `ERC1967Utils` precedent of using fixed unstructured storage slots that no future ABI extension can casually touch.
findings.impact: Funds attribution depends on `developer` and `factory` never changing. A future maintainer adds a 'migration helper' for legitimate reasons (compromised key recovery, treasury migration, etc.) and accidentally allows fund rerouting. The ABI-name-based test does not catch semantic violations. Single point of failure for the entire MVP's security model.
findings.recommendation: Either: (a) enforce via storage pattern — write `developer` and `factory` to unstructured storage slots (`keccak256('paymentvault.developer')`, etc.) and access only via internal getters; any future direct-storage writer would have to deliberately target the slot, making accidental mutation impossible; OR (b) introduce a stronger compile-time check: extract `developer`/`factory` reads into an `IPaymentVaultImmutables` interface, store the values in a separate `PaymentVaultImmutables` library/struct with a `view`-only accessor; OR (c) at minimum, expand the Task 5 test from 'ABI does not contain selector X' to 'storage write opcodes (`SSTORE`) targeting slot 0/1 appear at exactly two locations: `initialize`, end of preamble'. Document the structural invariant in NatSpec with a `/// @custom:security-contact` annotation and link to D17.
findings.cwe: CWE-1041 (Use of Redundant Code), CWE-665 (Improper Initialization)

findings.category: A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
findings.title: D18 logger payload schema is unspecified — leak vector via uncontrolled fields
findings.description:
D18 specifies the event NAMES but not the PAYLOADS for the 10 events. The spec says 'Payloads pass through the relayer-key redactor.' This is necessary but not sufficient. Concerns:

1. **PII / pseudonymous identifiers**: Events like `signature_invalid`, `nonce_replay_attempt`, `vault_not_deployed`, `network_mismatch` involve `authorization.from` — the payer agent's wallet address. Logging this systematically creates a tracking dataset of which agents tried to pay which developer, with timestamps. Under GDPR Recital 30 / Article 4(1), wallet addresses linked to natural persons can be personal data. No retention/redaction policy is specified.

2. **`settlement_failed` payload may include raw RPC errors**: RPC errors can contain raw transaction calldata which embeds the EIP-712 signature (v/r/s). The redactor as specified (32-byte hex) does NOT catch the full 65-byte signature (see CRITICAL finding above). Even with the regex fix, the *raw RPC error.cause chain* often serializes via `JSON.stringify(error)`, which can include the relayer's `request.account` field with `privateKey` shape.

3. **`relayer_initialized` payload undefined**: at startup, what fields go in the payload? If a future maintainer writes `securityEvent('relayer_initialized', config)`, the entire config including the `OpaqueRelayerKey` wrapper is serialized. The wrapper has redaction guards (D13), but the spec doesn't mandate which fields are passed.

4. **Default no-op logger**: hides production incidents in dev/test. The `forked-e2e` integration test SHOULD assert specific security events fired during specific failure paths, otherwise drift between spec and implementation is silent.

5. **Logger backpressure / synchronous throws**: if the integrator's `pino`/`winston` is misconfigured (transport down, disk full), a throwing `logger.securityEvent` call could crash the request path. The spec doesn't mandate `try/catch` around logger calls or async detachment.
findings.impact: Three concrete impacts: (a) GDPR/privacy compliance gap (logging payer wallet addresses without retention policy); (b) credential leak via uncontrolled payload field that the redactor misses; (c) self-DoS via misbehaving logger transport.
findings.recommendation:
Specify the per-event payload shape explicitly:
```ts
type SecurityEvent =
  | { name: 'signature_invalid'; payload: { from: string; reason: 'bad_v' | 'bad_recovered' | 'bad_domain'; networkId: string } }
  | { name: 'nonce_replay_attempt'; payload: { from: string; nonceHash: string /* SHA256 of nonce, not nonce */; networkId: string } }
  | { name: 'settlement_failed'; payload: { reason: SettlementReason; txHash?: string; chainId: number } }
  ...
```
State:
1. Payer wallet addresses (`from`) appear ONLY in events where attribution is operationally needed; document in README that operators are responsible for retention policy.
2. Nonces are logged as SHA256(nonce), not raw nonce — sufficient for correlating duplicates without enabling pre-image tracking.
3. `relayer_initialized` payload is `{ address: <relayer EOA>, network: string }` — explicitly no key, no config object.
4. Logger calls MUST be wrapped in try/catch in middleware; failures swallowed with a single stderr warning per process.
5. Add forked-e2e assertions: each failure-path test asserts the corresponding `securityEvent` fired with the spec'd payload shape.
findings.cwe: CWE-359 (Exposure of Private Personal Information), CWE-532 (Logging Sensitive Info), CWE-400 (Uncontrolled Resource Consumption via logger backpressure)

findings.title: D15 leaves a vault-impl bytecode-integrity gap (SELFDESTRUCT / DELEGATECALL invariant)
findings.description:
D15 disables initializer-based hijack of the impl, addressing the most common cloneable-contract risk. However: the spec doesn't lock the structural invariants of the impl bytecode itself. Two specific concerns:

1. **SELFDESTRUCT**: If any future revision of `PaymentVaultImpl` introduces a SELFDESTRUCT opcode (directly or via a library call), and the impl gets destructed (even pre-Cancun behavior on testnets), ALL deployed clones become non-functional — funds locked forever in clones that delegatecall to empty bytecode. Solidity 0.8.20 emits `SELFDESTRUCT` only via `selfdestruct(addr)` keyword, but inline assembly can still emit `0xff`. EIP-6049 deprecates but doesn't remove the opcode pre-Cancun; Arc Testnet behavior unverified.

2. **DELEGATECALL**: If the impl introduces DELEGATECALL, the clones execute it in their own storage context — an attacker who can influence the delegatecall target can drain the vault's USDC allowance / overwrite `developer` / overwrite `factory`. D17's 'no setter' invariant is moot if an attacker can write storage via delegatecall.

3. **External library calls**: OpenZeppelin's `SafeERC20.safeTransfer` is `library` code — Solidity compiles `using SafeERC20 for IERC20` as internal call (inlined), not DELEGATECALL — but if someone refactors to `address(safeERC20Library).delegatecall(...)`, the invariant breaks.
findings.impact: Funds permanently locked in clones (SELFDESTRUCT), or developer + factory back-pointer overwritten (DELEGATECALL). Both are catastrophic for funds in vaults. Current impl per spec doesn't have these, but no test/check prevents future drift.
findings.recommendation: Add an invariant test in Task 5: load the deployed `vaultImpl` runtime bytecode (`provider.getCode(vaultImpl)`), assert it contains NO `0xff` (SELFDESTRUCT) and NO `0xf4` (DELEGATECALL) opcodes outside the EIP-1167 proxy-shim region. Document the invariant in `PaymentVaultImpl.sol` NatSpec: '/// @custom:invariant No SELFDESTRUCT or DELEGATECALL in runtime bytecode — clones rely on impl being immutable.' Add a CI check that runs `slither --print human-summary` and asserts these opcodes absent. Also explicitly state in D15 NatSpec: the impl is bytecode-frozen post-deploy; migration requires a NEW factory + new clones.
findings.cwe: CWE-668 (Exposure of Resource to Wrong Sphere), CWE-672 (Operation on a Resource after Expiration or Release)

findings.title: D16 native-asset refusal doesn't prevent SELFDESTRUCT force-send — invariant statement is incomplete
findings.description:
D16 states that the vault has no `receive()` or `fallback()`, so native asset 'reverts'. This is true for ordinary transfers (`vault.call{value: x}`, `vault.transfer()`, `vault.send()`). It is NOT true for:

1. **`SELFDESTRUCT(vault)` from another contract**: pre-Cancun, this forces native asset to arrive without triggering receive/fallback. The vault accumulates native balance with no withdrawal path.
2. **Pre-deploy CREATE2 funding**: an attacker can send native asset to the predicted vault address BEFORE the developer calls `register()`. When `register()` deploys the clone, the clone inherits that pre-existing balance. Per EVM semantics, this does NOT block deployment (CREATE2 only checks code is empty, not balance).

The risk:
- D16 states 'balanceOf(this) is the only quantity that matters' as the invariant. This is correct because the vault's `withdraw()` only reads `usdc.balanceOf`. But the broader claim 'native sends prevent accidental loss' is wrong — accidental native loss via SELFDESTRUCT or pre-deploy funding is still possible, just locked permanently because there's no withdraw path for native.
- USDC on Arc is the native gas token — does this mean 'native asset' IS USDC? If so, the entire D16 framing is wrong: native USDC sent to the vault MUST be withdrawable, or accidental sends lock funds.

Need to clarify: on Arc, is USDC accessed exclusively via ERC-20 transfer semantics, or does it ALSO have native-asset semantics (`address.call{value: x}`) that the spec needs to address?
findings.location: Section: Decisions / D16 + Risks (Arc USDC dual-interface row)
findings.impact: (a) Permanently locked native asset in vault if forced via SELFDESTRUCT or pre-deploy funding (gas / bookkeeping nuisance, not catastrophic since `balanceOf` semantics ignore it). (b) Confusion about Arc's USDC-as-gas semantics: if native sends to vault are valid, the entire 'no receive/fallback' decision blocks legitimate use. Risks table already flags Arc dual-interface as a concern — D16 needs to align.
findings.recommendation: Resolve the Arc USDC semantics in Wave 1 Task 3 spike: explicitly test (i) does `vault.call{value: x}()` from an EOA succeed on Arc Testnet when USDC = native? (ii) does the vault's `usdc.balanceOf(this)` reflect that balance, or are native USDC and ERC-20 USDC two separate accounting systems on Arc? Document the answer in D16 with concrete behavior. If native sends ARE valid USDC sends on Arc, add a `receive() external payable {}` to the vault. If they're NOT (ERC-20 path only), state the SELFDESTRUCT / pre-deploy-funding edge case in D16's Rationale as 'accepted residual risk: small native balance can be force-sent and is permanently inaccessible — does not affect USDC withdrawals.'
findings.cwe: CWE-754 (Improper Check for Unusual Conditions), CWE-682 (Incorrect Calculation)

findings.category: A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
findings.title: D18 event set is missing `relayer_low_balance` and `chain_id_mismatch`
findings.description:
Two operational/security events are referenced elsewhere in the spec but not in the D18 enumeration:

1. **`relayer_low_balance`**: Risks table for 'Relayer wallet exhausts USDC mid-settle' specifies a pre-flight balance check returning `settlement_failed.reason = relayer_no_balance`. This SHOULD fire a distinct security event (proactive, not failure-after-the-fact) so operators get an early warning. D18's `settlement_failed` event covers post-failure, not pre-failure.

2. **`chain_id_mismatch`**: D14 specifies that on startup, `client.getChainId()` is compared and `NetworkMismatchError` is thrown on mismatch. Throwing terminates the process — but operators should ALSO see this as a security event before the throw, so it's distinguishable from generic startup crashes in monitoring.
findings.impact: Operational blindness: relayer running low on gas isn't surfaced until first payment fails; chain ID misconfig at startup looks like a generic crash. Both reduce mean-time-to-detect for the specific incident class.
findings.recommendation: Extend D18 event set to 11+ entries: add `relayer_low_balance` (payload: `{ relayerAddress, balance, threshold, networkId }`) and `chain_id_mismatch` (payload: `{ expectedChainId, actualChainId, rpcUrl: '[REDACTED]' }`). Wire `relayer_low_balance` into the pre-flight balance check in `settle.ts` (per Risks row). Wire `chain_id_mismatch` into D14 immediately before throwing `NetworkMismatchError`.

findings.title: D17 NatSpec / D18 documentation doesn't anchor to /// @custom:security-contact
findings.description: D15/D16/D17 each describe contract-level security invariants meant to be enforced socially (no setters, no receive, no re-init). These invariants live in the tech-spec but the Solidity code itself has no machine-readable annotation that links a future maintainer back to this spec. NatSpec supports `/// @custom:security-contact` and `/// @custom:invariant` annotations that show up in tooling (slither, mythx, web3 explorers).
findings.impact: Future maintainer reading the .sol file with no spec context may not know that absence of setters / receive / re-init is intentional. Adds a feature, breaks an invariant, security review catches it (or doesn't). Just-in-time documentation reduces drift risk over months.
findings.recommendation: Mandate in Task 4 description: each contract MUST carry `/// @custom:security-contact <project email>` and each enforced invariant from D15/D16/D17 MUST carry a `/// @custom:invariant <short description, ref to D-number>` NatSpec comment at the relevant function/storage declaration. Add a CI lint check (`solhint` with custom rule, or grep) that asserts these annotations exist.
